sutta: the verbal kotita the great mahakothita came to visit the verbal sariputta on coming to him he greeted him courteously etc etc and sat down at 1 side so seated the verbal maha kotita said this to the vulnerable sariputta your reverence or abuso does anything at all exist after the passionless ending without remainder of the 6 fears of contact and variable sarikuta said say not that your reverence and then said then your reverence not anything exists after the ending of the 6 years of contact and verbal sarikuta said say not that your reverence and again verbal mahakata said then there is then there both is and is not anything existing after the passionless ending without remainder of the 6 years of contact and verbal sorry putta said say not that your reverence then verbal mahakoti said your reverence when questioned thus is there anything remaining or is there not or is there and is there not or neither is there nor is there not anything existing after the ending of the 6 6 years of contact you reply say not that your reverence pray then how is the meaning of what i said to be regarded verbal sariputta said your reverence in saying that there is something left 1 makes difficulty where there is none in saying that there is there neither is nor is not anything left 1 makes difficulty where there is none so long your reverence as there is going to the 6 fears of contact for just so long is there a going to difficulty but your reverence by the passionless ending without remainder of the 6 fears of contact there is calming down of difficulty so i'll stop here on this on this particular suta but i'd like to see about this this utah which is that
commentary: this suta is talking about the state of nibana you know after the ending of the 6 years of contact is the ending of the sixth consciousness the sixth consciousness makes up the whole world the whole realm of existence and when the 6 uh consciousness stopped that is the state of nibana and here he's asking whether anything exists and sarikuta said don't say that and then he said and that means nothing exists and the rules it also don't say that and then he said then both something exists and does not exist and then rebel sarikuta said don't say that again and neither also don't say that so you see in the in the the buddha said there are 2 extremes the any annihilationist view and the eternalist view if you say nothing exists in the state of paris then the buddha said you are an annihilationist you say nothing exists if you say something exists and then in the state of nibana the buddha said you are an eternalist so uh it is very dangerous not to say something exists and something does not exist but then in the diga become dhammabhuta brahma become brahma buddha and so the ultimate state the buddha didn't say that nothing exists he also didn't say that something exists but he said that actually that the tatar gata is profound immeasurable unfathomable like the ocean this is mentioned in majima nikaya 72 and um this uh ultimate state now which the body calls and the the buddha calls the brahma bodhi or the brahma become the hindus call it brahma and also call it atman and i believe that those holy men who have reached a very high level and they understand that this ultimate state but they always say that it is something that cannot be actually described that's why the buddha calls it the unconditioned the uncreate using all negative terms you know because it cannot be perceived by the mind it cannot be cognized by the mind it can only be attained so because this is a something that is that we cannot know unless our mind stops working because as long as our mind is working and that means our active mind the world exists but it is only when the active mind stops there is a type of consciousness which the buddha calls the unsupported consciousness in our suttas and which may be similar to what the hindus call awareness pure awareness but then as is mentioned in this suta this is a very delicate subject and sometimes people including monks and can come to great differences and come to uh to have uh very strong views and uh can even split the sangha when we have fixed views about this so it's very uh dangerous so it's uh best if we approach this subject very carefully and [Music] and not to uh to to to see and that something exists or nothing exists
